## - SUPPORT SET BOTTLENECKS FOR - VIDEO TEXT REPRESENTATION LEARNING

## **Mandela Patrick** _[∗]_ **, Po-Yao Huang, Florian Metze & Andrea Vedaldi** _[∗]_ Facebook AI

{mandelapatrick,berniehuang,fmetze,vedaldi}@fb.com

**Alexander Hauptmann Yuki M. Asano** _[∗]_ **& João Henriques** Language Technologies Institute Visual Geometry Group Carnegie Mellon University University of Oxford alex@cs.cmu.edu {yuki,joao}@robots.ox.ac.uk

## ABSTRACT

The dominant paradigm for learning video-text representations – noise contrastive learning – increases the similarity of the representations of pairs of samples that are known to be related, such as text and video from the same sample, and pushes away the representations of all other pairs. We posit that this last behaviour is too strict, enforcing dissimilar representations even for samples that are semanticallyrelated – for example, visually similar videos or ones that share the same depicted action. In this paper, we propose a novel method that alleviates this by leveraging a generative model to naturally push these related samples together: each sample’s caption must be reconstructed as a weighted combination of other support samples’ visual representations. This simple idea ensures that representations are not overly-specialized to individual samples, are reusable across the dataset, and results in representations that explicitly encode semantics shared between samples, unlike noise contrastive learning. Our proposed method outperforms others by a large margin on MSR-VTT, VATEX, ActivityNet, and MSVD for video-to-text and text-to-video retrieval.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Noise contrastive learning (Gutmann & Hyvärinen, 2010) is emerging as one of the best approaches to learn data representations both for supervised (Khosla et al., 2020) and unsupervised regimes (Chen et al., 2020c). The idea is to learn a representation that discriminates any two data samples while being invariant to certain data transformations. For example, one might learn a representation that identifies a specific image up to arbitrary rotations (Misra & van der Maaten, 2020). In a multi-modal setting, the transformations can separate different modalities, for example, by extracting the audio and visual signals from a video. The resulting noise contrastive representation associates audio and visual signals that come from the same source video, differentiating others (Patrick et al., 2020).

The noise contrastive approach is motivated by the fact that the transformations that are applied to the data samples leave their ‘meaning’ unchanged. For example, rotating an image does not change the fact that it contains a cat or not (Gidaris et al., 2018). However, in most cases, we expect to find many data samples that share the same content without being necessarily related by simple transformations (e.g. think of any two images of cats). Existing noise contrastive formulations are unaware of these relationships and still try to assign different representations to these samples (Wu et al., 2018), despite the fact that they are semantically equivalent. If the representation is learned for a downstream task such as semantic video retrieval, this might degrade performance.
This suggest that there might be other learning signals that could complement and improve pure contrastive formulations. In this paper, we explore this idea in the case of learning from two modali-

ties: videos and text, in the form of video transcripts or captions. Given a state-of-the-art contrastive formulation that learns from these two modalities, we investigate complementary pretext objectives to improve it. First, we consider the _(instance) captioning_ task, namely mapping a video to the corresponding text, casting this as a conditional stochastic text generation problem. We show that this brings only a modest benefit.

We observe that the captioning task is highly sample-specific, as the goal is to produce a caption which describes a specific video and not any other video, and thus it suffers from the same disadvantages (discouraging concept sharing among samples) as contrastive learning. Thus, we propose to address this issue by switching to a different text generation task. The idea is to modify the text generator to take as input a learnable mixture of a support-set of videos, which we call _cross-instance captioning_ . The mixture weights are generated by comparing the learned video representations to captions’ representations in an online way over the batch. The limited set of support samples acts as a bottleneck that encourages extraction of shared semantics. In this manner, the embeddings can associate videos that share similar captions even if the contrastive loss tries to push them apart.

We show that, when the captioning task is added in this manner, it brings a sensible improvement to already very strong video representation learning results, further improving our own state-of-the-art baseline by a significant margin.

## 2 RELATED WORKS

Learning data representations from unlabelled data has been a long standing goal of machine learning. These approaches are called “self-supervised learning” because the learning signals, termed pretext tasks, are obtained from the data itself. In the image and video domain, pretext tasks include colorization (Zhang et al., 2016), rotation (Gidaris et al., 2018), or clustering (Asano et al., 2020a;b; Caron et al., 2018; Ji et al., 2018), while in the natural language domain, masked language modeling (Devlin et al., 2019), and next word prediction (Mikolov et al., 2013; Pennington et al., 2014) are extremely popular. These pretext tasks can be broadly classified into two classes: generative and discriminative.

Discriminative approaches learn representations by differentiating input samples, using objectives such as the contrastive loss (Gutmann & Hyvärinen, 2010; Hadsell et al., 2006). Discriminative approaches have proven to be particularly successful for image (Chen et al., 2020c; He et al., 2020; Misra & van der Maaten, 2020; Wu et al., 2018) and video (Han et al., 2019; Morgado et al., 2020; Patrick et al., 2020) representation learning. Generative approaches, on the other hand, try to reconstruct its input. GANs (Donahue & Simonyan, 2019; Goodfellow et al., 2014; Radford et al., 2015), autoencoders (Hinton & Salakhutdinov, 2006) and sequence-to-sequence models (Huang et al., 2020; Sutskever et al., 2014) are popular generative models. In this work, we show the importance of combining both discriminative and generative objectives to learn effective video-text representations.
The success of representation learning has also been due to advances in model architectures, such as the Transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017). BERT (Devlin et al., 2019) demonstrated that a transformer

architecture pretrained on large-scale textual data can learn transferable text representations that can be fine-tuned on a variety of downstream tasks. Subsequent works (Clark et al., 2020; Lewis et al., 2020a;b; Radford et al., 2019; Raffel et al., 2019) have improved upon the transformer architecture or training objective to learn even better representations. Inspired by the success of transformers in the NLP domain, several works have leveraged transformers to learn transferable image (Chen et al., 2020a; Desai & Johnson, 2020; Sariyildiz et al., 2020) or multi-modal image-text (Chen et al., 2019; Li et al., 2020a; 2019; Lu et al., 2019; Su et al., 2019; Tan & Bansal, 2019) and video-multilingual text (Huang et al., 2021) representations. In this work, we leverage the transformer architecture to better encode and represent text and video.

Large-scale training data has enabled the more effective pretraining of image (Sun et al., 2017; Yalniz et al., 2019), video (Ghadiyaram et al., 2019; Thomee et al., 2016) and textual representations (Raffel et al., 2019). The release of the HowTo100M dataset (Miech et al., 2019), a large-scale instructional video dataset, has spurred significant interest in leveraging large-scale pretraining to improve video-text representations for tasks such as video question-answering (Lei et al., 2018), text-video retrieval (Liu et al., 2019) and video captioning (Zhou et al., 2018b) on smaller datasets such as YouCookII (Zhou et al., 2018a), MSVD (Venugopalan et al., 2015a), MSR-VTT (Xu et al., 2016), LSMDC (Rohrbach et al., 2017), DiDeMo (Hendricks et al., 2018) and ActivityNet (Krishna et al., 2017). Although semantically rich and diverse, instructional videos from the web are super noisy and therefore a few approaches have been proposed to combat this. A few works (Luo et al., 2020; Sun et al., 2019a;b; Zhu & Yang, 2020) extend the BERT model to accept both visual and textual tokens to learn high-level semantic video-text representations. Other works have leveraged the contrastive loss (Miech et al., 2020) and show that using the raw audio (Alayrac et al., 2020; Rouditchenko et al., 2020) and other modalities (Gabeur et al., 2020) can be used to better align and improve video-text representations. While all these approaches rely on a contrastive objective, VidTranslate (Korbar et al., 2020) shows that a generative objective can also be used to learn joint video-text representations. In contrast to Korbar et al. (2020), we show that combining contrastive and generative objectives to pre-train video-text representations on large-scale data such as HowTo100M is very effective. The generative objective serves as regularizer to mitigate the strictness of the instance discrimination task of the constrastive objective, showing benefits similar to approaches such as clustering (Caron et al., 2020; Li et al., 2020b) and feature mixing (Kalantidis et al., 2020) which have been applied in the image domain.

## 3 METHOD

We consider the problem of learning multimodal representations from a corpus _C_ of video-text pairs ( _v, t_ ), where _v_ is a video and _t_ is its corresponding text (caption or transcription). Our goal is to learn a pair of representation maps _cv_ = Ψ( _v_ ) and _ct_ = Φ( _t_ ), with outputs in a _d_ -dimensional embedding space _cv, ct ∈_ R _[d]_ , where semantically similar instances are close to each other.

## 3.1 OBJECTIVE FOR LEARNING MULTIMODAL REPRESENTATIONS

We consider two learning objectives, also illustrated in Figure 1. The first is the contrastive objective, pushing embeddings _ct_ and _cv_ to be close if text _t_ and video _v_ come from the same sample and pushing them apart otherwise. This assumes that every sample is its own class and does not benefit from modelling similiarities _across_ instances. The second objective is generative captioning. In its most basic variant, it maximizes the probability of generating the text _t_ given the corresponding video _v_ . However, we suggest that variants that explicitly promote concept sharing between instances will result in better downstream performance, in tasks such as video retrieval. These variants, illustrated in Figure 2, have in common that the caption _t_ is reconstructed from a learned weighted combination over _other_ videos _v_ ˆ. This is a form of attention (Bahdanau et al., 2014) which encourages the network to learn about which videos share similar semantics, compensating for the contrastive loss and grouping them implicitly.

In the following, we denote with _B ⊂C_ a _batch_ of multi-modal samples, i.e. a finite collection of video-text pairs ( _t, v_ ) _∈C_ . For simplicity, we denote the batch as _B_ = _{_ ( _t[i] , v[i]_ ) _}[B] i_ =1 _[}]_[.]

## 3.1.2 CROSS-CAPTIONING OBJECTIVES

In the conventional captioning, the decoder seeks to optimize the negative log-likelihood of a text sequence _t_ given its corresponding video _v_ :

Here, the log-likelihood is obtained via auto-regressive decoding (Vaswani et al., 2017) from an intermediate video embedding _e[i] v_[= Φ] _[′]_[(] _[v][i]_[)][.][For the cross-captioning objective, we modify this loss] to condition the generation process on a weighted average of the embeddings of the _other_ videos in the batch, which we call the _support-set_ . The weights themselves, which can be interpreted as a batch-wise attention, are obtained as a softmax distribution with temperature _T_ over batch indices based on the video embeddings, as follows:

By default, the summation in the softmax is conducted over a support set _Si_ containing all indices except _i_ . This variant forces the network to extract all information required for captioning from other videos in the batch. Figure 2 compares graphically these attention mechanisms.

Considering both discriminative and generative objectives for learning multimodal representations, our full objective is _L_ = _L_[contrast] + _λL_[cross-captioning] _,_ where _λ_ balances two objectives. We set _λ_ = 10 to ensure similar magnitudes for both losses in our experiments. In the training phase, we use Adam (Kingma & Ba, 2015) to minimize our loss. At inference time, we directly use Φ( _t_ ) and Ψ( _v_ ) to encode video and text representations for retrieval.

## 3.2 MODEL ARCHITECTURE

We now discuss the details of the encoders and decoder components in our architecture, illustrated in fig. 2. For the _text decoder p_ ( _t|ev_ ) in eq. (2) and (3), we use a pre-trained T-5 decoder (Raffel et al., 2019).

For the _video representation cv_ = Ψ( _v_ ) = Ψ _[′′]_ (Ψ _[′]_ ( _v_ )), we use a video encoder _ev_ = Ψ _[′]_ ( _v_ ) followed by a multi-layer transformer pooling head _cv_ = Ψ _[′′]_ ( _ev_ ). The encoder Ψ _[′]_ ( _v_ ) concatenates the output of pretrained ResNet-152 (He et al., 2016) and R(2+1)D-34 (Tran et al., 2018) networks applied to individual video frames, resulting in a code _ev_ = [ _ev_ 1 _· · · evM_ ] where _M_ is the maximum duration of a video clip. For the pooling head _cv_ = Ψ _[′′]_ ( _ev_ ), we consider a transformer architecture to attend to important context and summarize it into a fixed-length representation _cv_ . For this, we follow MMT (Gabeur et al., 2020), but with two important differences. First, while MMT uses 7 expert features that results in 7 _×_ the sequence length, we only use a transformer to attend to early-fused motion and appearance features as the video representation, thus significantly reducing the sequence length and computational cost. Second, instead of stacking 6 transformer layers to encode the visual stream as in MMT, we only use a shallow two-layer transformer architecture with additional pre-encoders, further increasing model efficiency. As temporal 1D-convolutional neural networks (CNNs) (LeCun et al., 1998) were shown to effectively capture temporal dependencies in videos (Dong et al., 2019), we integrate CNNs into our transformer pooling heads to better capture video temporal signals. In more detail, we compute _cv_ = Ψ _[′′]_ ( _ev_ ) by chaining two transformer layers, each of the type:
_ψ_ ( _e_ ) = BN(FFN( _e_ attn) + _e_ attn) _, e_ attn = BN(MHA( _f_ ( _e_ )) + _f_ ( _e_ )) _._ (4)

Here _f_ is a pre-encoder that refines the video representation; we found empirically that a 1D CNN works well for this purpose. Then, we apply multi-head self-attention (MHA) (Huang et al., 2019; Vaswani et al., 2017) followed by a feed-forward network (FNN) with batch normalization (BN) (Ioffe & Szegedy, 2015). The architecture maps the input sequence _ev_ to a new ‘contextualized’ sequence of representation vectors; we take the first one as _cv_ .

The text representation decomposes in the same way as _ct_ = Φ( _t_ ) = Φ _[′′]_ (Φ _[′]_ ( _t_ )). The text encoder _et_ = Φ _[′]_ ( _t_ ) uses a pretrained T-5 network resulting in a code _et_ = [ _et_ 1 _· · · etN_ ], where _N_ is the maximum length of a sentence. The pooling head _ct_ = Φ _[′′]_ ( _et_ ) follows the same design as the video case, but _f_ is set to a recurrent neural network (RNN) instead of a CNN. Please refer to the appendix for details.
In practice, for computational reasons, we use eq. (3) to finetune the parameters of all networks except the video encoder Ψ _[′]_ ( _v_ ), which is fixed.

## 4 EXPERIMENTS

We validate empirically the ability of our method to learn better representations for the downstream tasks of text-to-video and video-to-text retrieval. First, in sec. 4.2 we ablate various model components on the MSR-VTT dataset. Then, in sec. 4.3 we show that our best model significantly outperforms state-of-the-art retrieval systems on three datasets, MSR-VTT, ActivtyNet and VATEX. Finally, in sec. 4.4 we analyse qualitatively the effect of the attention mechanism used during training.

4.1 EXPERIMENTAL SETUP

**Datasets. HowTo100M** (Miech et al., 2019) is a large-scale instructional video collection of 1.2 million YouTube videos, along with automatic speech recognition transcripts. We use this dataset for our pre-training experiments. **MSR-VTT** (Xu et al., 2016) contains 10,000 videos, where each video is annotated with 20 descriptions. We report results on the 1k-A split (9,000 training, 1,000 testing) as in Liu et al. (2019). **VATEX** (Wang et al., 2019) is a multilingual (Chinese and English) video-text dataset with 34,911 videos. We use the official training split with 25,991 videos and report on the validation split as in HGR (Chen et al., 2020b). The **ActivityNet Caption** (Krishna et al., 2017) dataset consists of densely annotated temporal segments of 20K YouTube videos. We use the 10K training split to train from scratch/ finetune the model and report the performance on the 5K ‘val1’ split. The **MSVD** (Chen & Dolan, 2011) dataset consists of 80 _K_ English descriptions for 1,970 videos from YouTube, with each video associated with around 40 sentences each. We use the standard split of 1,200, 100, and 670 videos for training, validation, and testing (Liu et al., 2019; Venugopalan et al., 2015b; Xu et al., 2015).
**Evaluation Metrics.** To measure the text-to-video and video-totext retrieval performance, we choose Recall at K (R@K) and Median Rank (MedR), which are common metrics in information retrieval.

**Video Encoder.** In Tab. 2a, we show the effect of the choice of visual input features. We find that for text-to-video retrieval at Recall at 1 and 5 ( _R_ @1 _, R_ @5), features obtained from a video R(2+1)D-34 ResNet achieve 2 _._ 9% and 7 _._ 0% higher performance compared to only image-frame based features from a ResNet-152. A further 3 _._ 5% and 2 _._ 0% can be gained by concatenating both features, yielding the strongest _MdR_ of 3 _._ 0%.

**Feature Aggregation.** While the features from both video and image-based visual encoders have reduced spatial extent after a fully-connected layer, the temporal dimension can be reduced in various ways. In Tab. 2b, we find that our multi-head, parameterized attention reduction yields strong gains over the mean- or max-pooling baselines of over 4% for _R_ @1. This shows that learning attention over the temporal dimension of fixed feature sets can give strong gains even without fine-tuning the encoder.

**Text Encoder.** In Tab. 2c, we find decent gains of 2 _._ 7% and 0 _._ 4% for R@1,5 for using T5-base, instead of T5-small. We do not use the T-5-Large model, as in Korbar et al. (2020), due to the prohibitively large relative model size increase of +220%.
**Text Decoder.** In Tab. 2d, we find that using a larger text decoder gives a 1% increase in performance when using the cross-captioning objective.

**Contrastive Loss.** To validate the choice of a triplet loss in eq. (1), in Tab. 2e, we compare the results of the InfoNCE contrastive loss (Oord et al., 2018) with a triplet loss, with both the intra and inter-intra modality variants. We find that InfoNCE (Oord et al., 2018) loss does not work well in our case, likely due to the difficulty in tuning this loss to have the right combination of temperature and batch-size.

**Support-Set Size.** Lastly, in Tab. 2f, we show the effect of the size of the support set used for cross-instance captioning. We find that our reconstruction loss indeed acts as a bottleneck, with both smaller and very large sizes degrading the performance.

**Captioning Objective.** In Tab. 1, we show the effect of the different variants of our learning objective eq. (3). First, we find that the naive addition of a reconstruction objective (“Identity”) does not improve the contrastive-only baseline (“None”) much. Considering reconstruction from other videos improves the performance more. In particular, the “Hybrid” variant, which combines “Identity” and “Full” (sec. 3.1.2) improves Recall at 1 and 5 from 25 _._ 9% and 53 _._ 0% to 26 _._ 0% and 54 _._ 8%, respectively. However, the best result by far (27 _._ 2 _/_ 55 _._ 2%) is obtained forcing captions to be reconstructed only from _other_ videos, via our cross-instance attention mechanism (“Cross”). This variant cannot use information contained in a video to generate the corresponding caption and thus entirely relies on the model to discover meaningful relationship between different videos. ## 4.3 COMPARISON TO STATE-OF-THE-ART

In this section, we compare the results of our method to other recent text-to-video and video-to-text retrieval approaches on various datasets. In Tab. 3 to 5, we show the results of our model applied to text-to-video and video-to-text retrieval on MSR-VTT, VATEX, ActivityNet and MSVD with and without pre-trainig on HowTo100M. Without pre-training, our method outperforms all others in all metrics and datasets. In particular, for the VATEX dataset, our retrieval performance at recall at 1 and 5 is 45 _._ 9% and 82 _._ 4%, exceeding recent state-of-the-art methods (Chen et al., 2020b) by a margin of 9%. For ActivityNet, our model outperforms MMT by a margin of 4% at recall at 1. With pre-training on HowTo100M, our performance further increases across the board. Notably, unlike MMT which uses 7 features, our model uses only 2 features and achieves state-of-the-art in most metrics.

## 4.4 ANALYSIS

In order to better understand the effect of our learning objective, we visualize the soft attention of our best-performing cross-instance reconstruction model in fig. 3. As we can see in the top-left square, which shows the pairwise attention between all pairs of videos in the batch, it is highly focused, with the model mostly attending one or two other instances in the batch.

8

For the first video’s caption reconstruction (second row), we find that the model solely attends to another musical performance video that is in the batch, ignoring the others. For the second video (third row), the model focuses on another sample that shows the sea but differs in most other aspects since there are no semantically-equivalent clips in the batch. The third video shares a similar scenario. These examples show that the bottleneck is effective at forcing the model to avoid memorising the video-caption association of each clip in isolation, and attempt to match other clips more broadly, since an exact (or very close) match is not guaranteed.

## 5 CONCLUSION

In this work, we studied classic contrastive learning methods such as the triplet loss to learn videotext representations for cross-model retrieval. We suggested that the contrastive approach might pull apart videos and captions even when they are semantically equivalent, which can hinder downstream retrieval performance. To mitigate this effect, we propose to consider a captioning pretext task as an additional learning objective. In particular, we show that cross-instance captioning can encourage the representation to pull together videos that share a similar caption, and are thus likely to be equivalent for retrieval. Leveraging these ideas, our model achieves state-of-the-art performance on the text-to-video and video-to-text retrieval tasks, on three datasets.

While we demonstrated these ideas in the specific case of text-to-video retrieval, they can in principle generalize to any setting that utilizes a contrastive loss, including self-supervised learning, provided that it is possible to learn reasonable conditional generators of a modality or data stream given another.

progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.label: Progressive (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: This paper presents a compelling and conceptually sound motivation for addressing the limitations of noise contrastive learning in multi-modal representation tasks, specifically by identifying the "false negatives" problem where semantically related samples are unnecessarily pushed apart. The writing is clear, logical, and provides a strong academic narrative that transitions effectively from the limitations of existing frameworks to the proposed "cross-instance captioning" solution. The methodology of using a support-set bottleneck as a mechanism to encourage the extraction of shared semantic concepts is elegant and well-motivated. However, the most significant risk in the subsequent sections of the paper will likely be the clarity of the implementation details; because the proposed "generative" mechanism involves a learned mixture of video representations, the authors must rigorously justify why this approach avoids collapse (a common failure mode in generative models) and clearly delineate the mathematical formulation of the "weighted combination" to ensure the results are reproducible. Overall, the foundational framing is excellent, positioning the work as a sophisticated contribution to the self-supervised learning landscape.

progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: The paper presents a compelling and well-motivated contribution that effectively addresses a critical limitation of noise contrastive learning: the overly strict penalization of semantically related negatives. The writing is clear and polished, with a persuasive problem statement regarding the over-specialization of embeddings and a logically structured distinction between instance-specific captioning and the proposed generative "cross-captioning" task. The concept of a support-set bottleneck to enforce shared semantics is intuitively sound, and the reported significant performance gains across multiple retrieval benchmarks suggest high empirical quality. However, the most significant issue lies in the clarity and justification of the support set mechanism itself; the authors must rigorously define how the support set is constructed, selected, and maintained, particularly regarding computational complexity and retrieval costs relative to standard batch-based methods. Furthermore, the novelty of the soft-weighted reconstruction over a support set requires sharper differentiation from existing memory-bank or clustering-based approaches that already mitigate the "push-away" problem; the manuscript should include a deeper analysis of bottleneck hyperparameter sensitivity and demonstrate that the gains stem specifically from the semantic sharing enforced by the bottleneck rather than merely the increased effective sample diversity or the addition of a reconstruction signal.

comments.title: Missing Triplet Loss Formulation
comments.quote:
Following Faghri et al. (2018), we adopt the hinge-based triplet ranking loss with hard negative mining:

where α is the correlation margin between positive and negative pairs and [·]+ = max{0, ·} is the hinge function.
comments.explanation: Initially, I expected to see the explicit mathematical formulation for the triplet loss here, but the equation is completely absent. I checked the surrounding text and section references to see if it was defined elsewhere, but it is not. While this is almost certainly an OCR artifact where a mathematical block was dropped during PDF extraction, the loss formulation is central to the methodology. Without the equation, a reader cannot verify how the hinge function is applied, how positive and negative similarities are ordered (e.g., s(pos, neg) - s(pos, pos) + margin), or how 'hard negative mining' is dynamically implemented. This leaves the training objective formally incomplete and difficult to reproduce.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Attention Query Source
comments.quote: The mixture weights are generated by comparing the learned video representations to captions’ representations in an online way over the batch. ... based on the video embeddings, as follows:
comments.explanation: When reading the introduction, I initially assumed the attention mechanism used cross-modal alignment, as it explicitly mentions 'comparing the learned video representations to captions’ representations.' This is a common and intuitive setup for retrieval tasks. However, when I moved to the formal method section (3.1.2), it stated the weights are obtained 'based on the video embeddings.' This creates a genuine ambiguity: does the support-set selection rely on text-video similarity (cross-modal) or visual-video similarity (intra-modal)? Since this fundamentally changes the information flow and the 'bottleneck' semantics, the discrepancy needs to be clarified to ensure readers understand the exact mechanism being proposed.

comments.title: Non-Standard Definition of Hybrid Captioning Weights
comments.quote: Hybrid captioning sets the weights in eq. (3) as the average of the weights for identity captioning and full support.
comments.explanation: I initially interpreted 'hybrid' as a learned routing mechanism or a mixture of experts. Upon closer inspection, the authors define it as simply averaging the softmax weights from the identity and full-support variants. In attention literature, directly averaging softmax outputs is non-standard and can yield problematic distributions (e.g., overly flattened probabilities or unintended multimodal peaks), as it does not preserve the log-probability geometry that softmax is designed for. Conventionally, hybrid or mixture approaches average logits or feature representations before applying the softmax operation. I recommend the authors clarify if this is an intentional heuristic or if they meant to average the pre-softmax values, as the current definition could lead to unexpected attention behavior.

comments.title: Typographical error in support set definition for Cross-captioning
comments.quote: **Cross-captioning** ( _Si_ = _{j_ = _i}_ ) considers all _but_ the video that one wishes to caption.
comments.explanation: The authors define the support set for Cross-captioning as Si = {j = i}, but immediately clarify that it 'considers all *but* the video that one wishes to caption.' This is a direct contradiction. Given the context of enforcing cross-instance reliance and the definitions of other variants (Identity uses {i}, Full uses {1,...,B}), the intended set is undoubtedly Si = {j ≠ i}. This appears to be a clear typo (likely an author slip or OCR artifact where ≠ was rendered as =).

comments.title: Underspecified finetuning procedure and incorrect equation reference
comments.quote: In practice, for computational reasons, we use eq. (3) to finetune the parameters of all networks except the video encoder Ψ _[′]_ ( _v_ ), which is fixed.
comments.explanation: Equation (3) in the provided text defines the softmax attention weights for the support set, not the training objective or loss function. Stating that they 'use eq. (3) to finetune' is methodologically incorrect. Furthermore, the phrase 'for computational reasons' strongly implies that the authors may be dropping the contrastive loss during the finetuning phase and only optimizing the generative objective, but this critical methodological choice is not explicitly stated. It leaves unclear whether the full combined objective L = L_contrast + λL_cross-captioning is used or only the cross-captioning part, and which specific parameters are updated. This ambiguity could mislead readers attempting to reproduce the finetuning setup.

comments.quote: In this work, we studied classic contrastive learning methods such as the triplet loss to learn videotext representations for cross-model retrieval.
comments.explanation: I initially spotted 'cross-model retrieval' in the conclusion and wondered if this implied a multi-model architecture or a different evaluation setup than what was described. Cross-referencing the abstract, Figure 1 caption, and Section 4.2, where the authors consistently use 'cross-modal retrieval', it is clear this is a typographical slip. The surrounding context makes the intended meaning unambiguous, so this is a minor terminology inconsistency that does not affect the technical substance.

comments.title: Dataset abbreviation inconsistency: 'VTT' vs 'MSR-VTT'
comments.quote: Attention scores of all pairs in a batch (topleft square) and a subset of rows/columns (other squares) on VTT.
comments.explanation: The figure caption states the visualization is 'on VTT'. In Section 4.1 (Experimental Setup), the dataset is formally introduced and consistently referred to as 'MSR-VTT'. While 'VTT' is almost certainly intended as a shorthand for MSR-VTT, the abbreviation breaks the paper's established naming convention. A careful reader might briefly question whether a different dataset, split, or benchmark was used here, but the broader context readily resolves this. I flag it as a minor notation inconsistency for precision.

progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Progressive (deepseek-v4-flash)
progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: The paper presents a compelling and clearly motivated approach that addresses a key limitation of noise contrastive learning for video-text representation: its tendency to push apart semantically related samples. The proposed cross-instance captioning with a support-set bottleneck is a novel and well-articulated idea, and the writing is generally clear, with a strong logical flow from problem statement to solution. However, the initial excerpt lacks sufficient detail on the mechanics of the support-set weighting and how the generative and contrastive losses are balanced, which may raise concerns about the method's sensitivity to hyperparameters and batch composition. Additionally, while the paper claims significant improvements over baselines, no experimental results or ablation studies are presented in the provided text, making it impossible to assess the validity or magnitude of these gains. A more thorough preview of the empirical validation would strengthen the reader's confidence in the proposed framework.

comments.title: Typo in 'semanticallyrelated'
comments.quote: enforcing dissimilar representations even for samples that are semanticallyrelated – for example,
comments.explanation: The word 'semanticallyrelated' appears to be missing a space, likely due to a line-break or OCR issue. The intended reading is clearly 'semantically related', and this does not affect the technical content or understanding.

comments.title: Inconsistent subscript in cross-captioning objective equation
comments.quote: By default, the summation in the softmax is conducted over a support set Si containing all indices except i.
comments.explanation: The paper defines the support set S_i for cross-captioning as containing all indices except i. In equation (3), the softmax denominator sums over support set S_i, and the weight w_ij is defined for j in S_i. However, the 'Cross-captioning' variant is explicitly defined with S_i = {j ≠ i}, which is consistent. The potential confusion arises because the 'Full support' variant is defined as S_i = {1, ..., B}, which includes i itself, and 'Hybrid captioning' averages identity and full weights. The text clearly enumerates each variant, so a careful reader can resolve which form applies. No actual mathematical error exists, only a slight risk of ambiguous phrasing when reading quickly. The authors correctly distinguish the variants.

comments.title: Missing definition of T in softmax temperature
comments.quote: The weights themselves, which can be interpreted as a batch-wise attention, are obtained as a softmax distribution with temperature T over batch indices based on the video embeddings, as follows:
comments.explanation: The paper introduces a temperature parameter T in the softmax for computing attention weights in equation (3), but never specifies its value or how it is set (e.g., learned, fixed, annealed). This is a mild underspecification. However, in context, this is part of the method description, and the value might be given later in the appendix or experimental setup. Given that the paper is a conference paper with a strict page limit, such a parameter is often set to 1 or a small constant (e.g., 0.1) without detailed justification. This does not prevent reproduction for most practitioners, who can tune it, but it is an underspecified detail that could be flagged as a minor issue.

comments.title: Missing definition of λ value and balancing rationale
comments.quote: We set λ = 10 to ensure similar magnitudes for both losses in our experiments.
comments.explanation: The authors state they set λ=10 to balance loss magnitudes. However, they do not report the raw magnitudes of L_contrast and L_cross-captioning before scaling, nor do they explain how the factor 10 was chosen (e.g., grid search, heuristic). While this is a common hyperparameter choice, providing no justification or ablation on λ is a minor omission. The paper includes extensive ablations on other components, so this missing detail is noticeable but not critical.

comments.title: Inconsistency in term 'cross-instance captioning' vs 'cross-captioning'
comments.quote: which we call cross-instance captioning. ... Cross-captioning (Si = {j = i})
comments.explanation: The introduction refers to the method as 'cross-instance captioning', but in the method section (3.1.2) and throughout the paper, the primary term becomes 'cross-captioning'. The two terms are used interchangeably, which could confuse a reader who expects them to refer to different variants. However, the definition in section 3.1.2 makes clear that cross-captioning specifically excludes the self-index, so the names are consistent in meaning. This is a minor terminology inconsistency.

comments.title: Potential overclaim in abstract about outperforming 'others'
comments.quote: Our proposed method outperforms others by a large margin on MSR-VTT, VATEX, ActivityNet, and MSVD for video-to-text and text-to-video retrieval.
comments.explanation: The abstract claims the method outperforms 'others by a large margin'. While the experimental results in the paper (table 2, etc.) show improvements over baselines, the phrase 'others' is vague — does it refer to all prior methods, or only the contrastive baseline? In conference papers, such claims are common but can be overreaching if not all competitive methods are included in comparisons. The paper does compare to state-of-the-art methods in section 4.3, and the gains are meaningful, so this is a framing concern rather than a factual error. It is a minor exaggeration typical of abstract statements.

comments.title: Notation inconsistency: video encoder mislabeled as Φ'
comments.quote: Here, the log-likelihood is obtained via auto-regressive decoding (Vaswani et al., 2017) from an intermediate video embedding $e[i]_v = \Phi'(v_i)$.
comments.explanation: In Section 3, the text encoder is defined as Φ and the video encoder as Ψ. Later in Section 3.2, the video encoder is correctly denoted Ψ' and the text encoder Φ'. However, in Section 3.1.2, the video embedding is written as Φ'(v_i), which would imply applying the text encoder to the video. This is inconsistent with the established notation and with the rest of the method. It likely should be Ψ'(v_i). The error is not resolved by context because Φ' is later explicitly used for text encoding; a careful reader would be confused about which encoder is being used for video.

comments.title: Typographical error: 'constrastive' instead of 'contrastive'
comments.quote: the generative objective serves as regularizer to mitigate the strictness of the instance discrimination task of the constrastive objective
comments.explanation: The word 'constrastive' is a clear typo for 'contrastive'. This is a minor spelling error that does not affect comprehension, but it could be a sign of OCR errors in the paper. Since it appears in a technical term, it is worth noting.

comments.title: Ambiguity in training procedure: video encoder freezing
comments.quote: In practice, for computational reasons, we use eq. (3) to finetune the parameters of all networks except the video encoder Ψ'(v), which is fixed.
comments.explanation: The sentence states that when using eq. (3) (cross-captioning loss), the video encoder Ψ'(v) is fixed. However, the full objective is defined as L = L_contrast + λ L_cross-captioning, which suggests joint optimization of both losses. If the video encoder is frozen during this joint training, the contrastive loss can only update text-side components, limiting video representation learning. The paper does not clarify whether this freezing applies only to a separate fine-tuning stage (e.g., after contrastive pretraining) or to the entire training procedure. This ambiguity could mislead readers about which parameters are actually trained and affect reproducibility. The authors likely intend a two-stage approach (pretrain video encoder contrastively, then fine-tune with cross-captioning while freezing it), but the text as written appears to conflict with the full objective definition.

comments.title: Unclear token for video representation pooling
comments.quote: we compute cv = Ψ''(ev) by chaining two transformer layers ... The architecture maps the input sequence ev to a new 'contextualized' sequence of representation vectors; we take the first one as cv.
comments.explanation: The text describes taking the first vector of the contextualized sequence as the global video representation. In standard transformer pooling, a special [CLS] token is typically prepended to the sequence, and its output is used as the aggregate representation. Without such a token, the first vector corresponds to the first frame's feature after contextualization, which may not adequately summarize the entire video. The authors do not mention adding a learnable token or using any other pooling mechanism (e.g., mean pooling or attention pooling). This omission could lead to suboptimal representation and makes it unclear whether the first token corresponds to a special token or the first input element. Readers attempting to reproduce the method might misinterpret the pooling strategy.

comments.title: Notation inconsistency for feed-forward network
comments.quote: followed by a feed-forward network (FNN) with batch normalization
comments.explanation: In the same paragraph, the equation uses 'FFN' (feed-forward network) but the text parenthetically defines it as 'FNN'. This is a minor inconsistency in notation that could cause momentary confusion, though it is unlikely to mislead a careful reader since the intended meaning is clear.

comments.title: Numerical inconsistency in Text Encoder ablation
comments.quote: In Tab. 2c, we find decent gains of 2.7% and 0.4% for R@1,5 for using T5-base, instead of T5-small.
comments.explanation: The text states a gain of 0.4% for R@5 when switching from T5-small to T5-base, but Table 2c shows R@5 values of 51.2 (T5-small) and 55.2 (T5-base), a gain of 4.0 percentage points. The discrepancy is too large to be explained by rounding; it appears to be a typo or OCR error. This could mislead a reader about the benefit of a larger text encoder.

comments.title: Overclaim of state-of-the-art performance without pre-training
comments.quote: Without pre-training, our method outperforms all others in all metrics and datasets.
comments.explanation: This statement appears in the comparison to state-of-the-art section. While our method (without pre-training) indeed outperforms competitors on most metrics across the four datasets, it is not true for all metrics. On MSR-VTT video-to-text retrieval, Table 3 shows that MMT (without pre-training) achieves higher R@5 (56.0 vs. 55.1) and R@10 (67.8 vs. 67.5) than our method. Thus the claim of outperforming 'all others in all metrics' is factually incorrect for this dataset and modality combination. The claim should be qualified.

comments.title: Underspecified support-set size variation with memory bank
comments.quote: In Tab. 2f, we show the effect of the size of the support set used for cross-instance captioning. We find that our reconstruction loss indeed acts as a bottleneck, with both smaller and very large sizes degrading the performance.
comments.explanation: Table 2f includes a 'Memory bank' column with sizes 2k and 8k, yet the preceding method section (Section 3.1.2) only defines the support set as the current batch, with weights computed via softmax over batch indices. The memory bank is not introduced or explained in the provided context; it is unclear how it is constructed or used in the cross-captioning objective. A reader cannot reproduce this ablation without additional details. While the memory bank may be described elsewhere in the full paper, the omission in this passage leaves a gap in understanding a key ablation result.

comments.title: No issues found
comments.explanation: The passage is a qualitative analysis of attention maps, consistent with the method description. No mathematical, notation, or logical errors are present. The claims are appropriately supported by examples.

progressive__glm-4.7-flash.label: Progressive (glm-4.7-flash)
progressive__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: This manuscript addresses a relevant limitation in noise contrastive learning (NCL) for video-text representation learning: the tendency of standard NCL to enforce forced dissimilarity between semantically similar samples. The authors propose a novel "support set bottleneck" mechanism that alleviates this by introducing a generative task—cross-captioning—which reconstructs a caption as a weighted combination of visual representations from a support set of other samples. The writing quality is high, with a clear and persuasive motivation that effectively contrasts the discriminative nature of NCL with the more flexible, semantic alignment offered by the proposed generative approach. However, the most significant issues concern the definition and implementation of the support set bottleneck. The current text does not sufficiently clarify how the support set is constructed (e.g., is it an online mixture of batch samples or a

comments.title: Mismatch between Introductory description and Methodological definition of weight generation
comments.quote: The mixture weights are generated by comparing the learned video representations to captions’ representations in an online way over the batch. [Section 1] ... The weights themselves, which can be interpreted as a batch-wise attention, are obtained as a softmax distribution with temperature T over batch indices based on the video embeddings [Section 3.1.2]
comments.explanation: The introduction describes the mixture weights as being generated by comparing video and caption representations, which implies a cross-modal attention mechanism where captions act as queries. However, the formal definition in Section 3.1.2 (Equation 3 description) states the weights are based solely on video embeddings. While the contrastive loss ensures the video embeddings are aligned with the text, the weight generation mechanism described is a unimodal batch-attention (softmax over video embeddings), not a cross-modal comparison. This discrepancy could mislead a reader expecting a cross-modal retrieval-based support set selection.

comments.title: Incorrect Attribution of Pre-training Performance
comments.quote: Without pre-training, our method outperforms all others in all metrics and datasets. In particular, for the VATEX dataset, our retrieval performance at recall at 1 and 5 is 45.9% and 82.4%
comments.explanation: The text explicitly states that these results (45.9% R@1, 82.4% R@5) are achieved 'Without pre-training'. However, Table 4 (in the provided context) shows that the 'Ours-pretrained' row achieves exactly 45.9% R@1 and 82.4% R@5, while the 'Ours' (non-pretrained) row achieves 44.6% R@1 and 81.8% R@5. The text incorrectly attributes the performance metrics of the 'Ours-pretrained' configuration to the 'Without pre-training' configuration.

comments.title: Confusing State-of-the-Art Comparison on ActivityNet
comments.quote: For ActivityNet, our model outperforms MMT by a margin of 4% at recall at 1.
comments.explanation: The text claims a 4% margin over 'MMT' at R@1. In Table 5, 'MMT (Gabeur et al., 2020)' is listed at 22.7%, and 'Ours' is 26.8%. The difference is approximately 4%. However, the table also lists 'MMT-pretrained' at 28.7%, which actually outperforms 'Ours' (26.8%). Since the comparison is labeled 'Comparison to State-of-the-Art' and 'MMT-pretrained' is available in the table, claiming 'our model outperforms MMT' is misleading; the model under discussion fails to outperform the pretrained version of the cited baseline. The 4% margin only holds if comparing to the non-pretrained MMT, which is not the de facto SOTA benchmark when a pretrained variant is present.
